rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF LUIS URIBE
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #4, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 1/11/23. No earlier availability. There is no accounting attached to the amended pleading filed on 11/23/22. An amended pleading completely restates and supersedes the pleading it amends for all purposes. [CRC 7.3] An amendment to the amended petition must be filed and notice of the amendment must be served. (Completely restating and superseding means that an amended petition should stand on its own and not refer to costs "in question in the instant matter." Any costs not adequately described should simply be fully described. Here, the attached copy of the courier invoice is ok. There is no need to disclose in a petition that a proposed order has been lodged. That is not a substantive disclosure. A proposed order should simply be provided to the court.) A courtesy copy of the amendment and of the proof of service must be received by the examiner by 1/3/23 to avoid another continuance. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10.B.

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF CHUN JA ELLIS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #3, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 1/31/2023. Counsel to supplement the following information: 1) Original bill statements for the facility. Prob. Code Sec. 2620(c)(3); 2)Statements showing ending balances. Prob. Code Sec. 2620(c)(2); 3) During this accounting period, there were three lump sum disbursements. Further information is needed as to its nature, frequency, and authority; 4) Need allegation/ explanation as to split of expenses and costs. Some disbursements are being split between the conservatorship estate and the trust, such as Board & Care, newspaper subscription, property tax (Toulumne). Unable to find any regularity on medical expenses, property taxes, boarding care payment, entertaining expenses, and others. Comingling appears inappropriate; 5) Proposed order; and 6) proof of assessment fee payment (statement dated 12/7/22).

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF ZETHER MCGRIGER
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 1/17/23. (No earlier availability.) The second amended petition was filed on 11/28/22 (but not actually scanned until 12/1/22, when it first became available for viewing). Mailing notice before the filing (e-filing) of the pleading requiring notice is defective. Notice of the hearing of the second amended petition on 1/17/23 must be mailed at least 15 days before that hearing date, or waivers of notice by all interested parties must be filed. The assignment was for $75,800, not $78,500. (NB: Paragraph 7 still contains an optional allegation; however, it appears that notice was given to the DHCS. Prob. C. Sect. 9202(b) requires notice if an heir or BENEFICIARY is or has previously been confined in a prison or facility under the jurisdiction of the Department of Corrections, etc. It appears that Executor never marshaled Decedent's Bank of America account, as it remains the same number and titled in the name of the decedent. This Court has no jurisdiction over assets in Texas; the Court will not make any ruling in connection with actions Executor takes to deal with Texas real property.) Although an amendment should be filed to correct the incorrect assignment amount, as the executor is the primary beneficiary of this estate, after notice is corrected, this Court will not require that an amendment be filed. Courtesy copies of the proof of service (or waivers of notice) and any papers filed to address these issues must be received by the assigned Examiner by no later than 1/6/23. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: **TRANSFERRED TO SAN DIEGO COUNTY**IN THE MATTER OF THE TERESITA H. RAVETTI SEPARATE PROPERTY TRUST AGREEMENT
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition To Remove A Co-Trustee And Compel An Accounting
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by the bench officer, Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. 1. Venue The Petitioners are ordered to show cause why this court is a proper venue for their Petition to (1) Remove the Co-Trustee and Compel an Accounting, filed May 31, 2022, as amended (and renamed) Sept. 9, 2022 ("Petition"). The record shows that this court is not a proper venue for this action. Jurisdiction and venue in trust litigation are governed by Division 9 (Trust Law), Part 5 (Judicial Proceedings Concerning Trusts), Chapter 1 (Jurisdiction and Venue) of the Probate Code. A California court has subject matter jurisdiction over trust actions and proceedings and has personal jurisdiction over a trustee who "accept[s] the trusteeship of a trust having its principal place of administration in this state" and over beneficiaries of a trust with its principal place of administration in this state." (Prob. Code Sections 17003-17004.) Personal jurisdiction, however, is subject to constitutional limits and the State's long-arm statute. (Prob. Code Sect. 17004.) Here, the court appears to have subject-matter jurisdiction over the Petition and personal jurisdiction over the trustee and beneficiaries, at least "to the extent of [the beneficiaries'] interests in the trust." (See Prob. Code Sect. 17003, subd. (b).) Jurisdiction is properly exercised by a particular court, however, only if venue lies in that county. Statutes designate which county superior court is "the" or "a" proper court for trying the action. (Battaglia Enterprises, Inc. v. Superior Court (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 309, 313.) In the case of a living trust, like this one, the proper county for commencement of a trust proceeding is "the county where the principal place of administration of the trust is located." (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (a)(1).) "The principal place of administration of the trust is the usual place where the day-to-day activity of the trust is carried on by the trustee or its representative who is primarily responsible for the administration of the trust." (Prob. Code Section 17002, subd. (a).) The record shows that, at the time of commencement of this proceeding by the filing of the Petition, the principal place of administration of the Trust was San Diego. The record shows that Helen Omstead is a co-trustee that she is the principal administrating co-trustee and she performs her duties principally in San Diego. "If the principal place of administration of the trust cannot be determined under subdivision (a), it shall be determined as follows: [Paragraph] (1) If the trust has a single trustee, the principal place of administration of the trust is the trustee's residence or usual place of business[;] [Paragraph] (2) If the trust has more than one trustee, the principal place of administration of the trust is the residence or usual place of business of any of the co-trustees as agreed upon by them or, if not, the residence or usual place of business of any of the cotrustees." (Prob. Code Section 17002, subd. (b).) If a living trust has no trustee, then the situs of property controls. (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (b).) In cases not covered by these trust specific rules, the venue rules for civil actions generally apply. (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (c).) In this case, it does not appear that subsections (b) and (c) have any application because the records demonstrates that the principal place of administration can be determined and it is San Diego. A hearing on the OSC will take place 1/25/23. Any response to the OSC must be presented in writing and filed no later than 1/11/23, courtesy copies of all such filings must be submitted to the court no later than 1/18/23 (at least a week before the hearing).

rulings.Case Title: **TRANSFERRED TO SAN DIEGO COUNTY**IN THE MATTER OF THE TERESITA H. RAVETTI SEPARATE PROPERTY TRUST AGREEMENT
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION TO TRANSFER to SAN DIEGO County
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by the bench officer, Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. 1. Venue The Petitioners are ordered to show cause why this court is a proper venue for their Petition to (1) Remove the Co-Trustee and Compel an Accounting, filed May 31, 2022, as amended (and renamed) Sept. 9, 2022 ("Petition"). The record shows that this court is not a proper venue for this action. Jurisdiction and venue in trust litigation are governed by Division 9 (Trust Law), Part 5 (Judicial Proceedings Concerning Trusts), Chapter 1 (Jurisdiction and Venue) of the Probate Code. A California court has subject matter jurisdiction over trust actions and proceedings and has personal jurisdiction over a trustee who "accept[s] the trusteeship of a trust having its principal place of administration in this state" and over beneficiaries of a trust with its principal place of administration in this state." (Prob. Code Sections 17003-17004.) Personal jurisdiction, however, is subject to constitutional limits and the State's long-arm statute. (Prob. Code Sect. 17004.) Here, the court appears to have subject-matter jurisdiction over the Petition and personal jurisdiction over the trustee and beneficiaries, at least "to the extent of [the beneficiaries'] interests in the trust." (See Prob. Code Sect. 17003, subd. (b).) Jurisdiction is properly exercised by a particular court, however, only if venue lies in that county. Statutes designate which county superior court is "the" or "a" proper court for trying the action. (Battaglia Enterprises, Inc. v. Superior Court (2013) 215 Cal.App.4th 309, 313.) In the case of a living trust, like this one, the proper county for commencement of a trust proceeding is "the county where the principal place of administration of the trust is located." (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (a)(1).) "The principal place of administration of the trust is the usual place where the day-to-day activity of the trust is carried on by the trustee or its representative who is primarily responsible for the administration of the trust." (Prob. Code Section 17002, subd. (a).) The record shows that, at the time of commencement of this proceeding by the filing of the Petition, the principal place of administration of the Trust was San Diego. The record shows that Helen Omstead is a co-trustee that she is the principal administrating co-trustee and she performs her duties principally in San Diego. "If the principal place of administration of the trust cannot be determined under subdivision (a), it shall be determined as follows: [Paragraph] (1) If the trust has a single trustee, the principal place of administration of the trust is the trustee's residence or usual place of business[;] [Paragraph] (2) If the trust has more than one trustee, the principal place of administration of the trust is the residence or usual place of business of any of the co-trustees as agreed upon by them or, if not, the residence or usual place of business of any of the cotrustees." (Prob. Code Section 17002, subd. (b).) If a living trust has no trustee, then the situs of property controls. (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (b).) In cases not covered by these trust specific rules, the venue rules for civil actions generally apply. (Prob. Code Section 17005, subd. (c).) In this case, it does not appear that subsections (b) and (c) have any application because the records demonstrates that the principal place of administration can be determined and it is San Diego. A hearing on the OSC will take place 1/25/23. Any response to the OSC must be presented in writing and filed no later than 1/11/23, courtesy copies of all such filings must be submitted to the court no later than 1/18/23 (at least a week before the hearing).

Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. For an audio-only appearance through Zoom, call 1-669-254-5252 and key in meeting ID "160 225 4765#", then participant ID "0#", then password "514879#". (Toll rates may apply).) The Court prefers that counsel and parties appearing by video input their first and last name into the "Your Name" dialogue box. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling 1-888-882-6878. Counsel or parties appearing in person must wear a mask covering their nose and mouth at all times while in the courthouse. Please review LRSF 14.1(D) for more information.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JILL FRANCES GRIFFIN
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION FOR PROBATE OF WILL AND FOR LETTERS TESTAMENTARY
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 1/17/23. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: Notice of a petition to admit a LOST will must clearly indicate that the petitioner is seeking to admit a LOST will. [LRFS 14.20.] The lost will does not appear to have been lodged yet. No will file was provided to the examiner. Item 3h(2) of the petition appears to be incorrect. When second generation or more distant heirs are listed, the deceased ancestor through whom they take (or would have taken) shall be named and his or her date of death and relationship to the decedent shall be stated. [LRSF 14.18 C. and D.] Who were Decedent's parents and grandparents and when did they die, and who was the aunt or uncle who was the predeceased parent of the only cousin listed on Attachment 8? Were there any "written instructions provided" to the proposed executor? If so, the document(s) must be filed and any beneficiaries named in the written instructions must be listed and must receive notice. Courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues must be received by the assigned Examiner by no later than 1/6/23. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.
